setting.note: Two Uzbekistans held in one mind. In 1938 the writer Abdulla Qodiriy is taken to the NKVD prison in Tashkent during Stalin's terror, and in his cell he builds, sentence by sentence, an unwritten novel about the last khans of Kokand and the poet-queen Nodira. The map runs from the old city of Tashkent down some two hundred and thirty kilometres to the Ferghana Valley, where the nineteenth-century strand plays out in Kokand, Margilan and Ferghana.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Hamid Ismailov, first published in Uzbek as Jinlar bazmi and in English as The Devils' Dance, 2016. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Devils' Dance, Hamid Ismailov (2016). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The burnt terracotta of Ferghana brick and the khans' palace. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
